questions.scenario: The cluster autoscaler adds `worker-f` to the application node pool during a campaign. The platform needs a log collector on that node without changing an application replica count or editing a list of node names. Which workload best matches the requirement?

options.text: A DaemonSet whose placement rules make `worker-f` eligible, allowing the controller to add one node-local agent Pod.
A Deployment with a fixed replica count equal to the current number of nodes.
A CronJob that starts a new long-running collector on every schedule.
A StatefulSet whose ordinal number is manually mapped to each node name.

questions.explanation: A DaemonSet follows eligible nodes and adds an agent as the node set changes. A fixed Deployment count does not guarantee one Pod per node. CronJobs create finite scheduled work, and StatefulSet ordinals provide stable member identity rather than node coverage.

questions.scenario: Four application nodes are Ready, but the DaemonSet reports `DESIRED 3`, `CURRENT 3`, and `READY 3`. The missing node `worker-d` has label `devpolaris.io/node-pool=general`, while the DaemonSet selects `devpolaris.io/node-pool=app`. What is the best first repair direction?

options.text: Increase a `replicas` field to four because DaemonSet desired count is manually configured.
Correct the node-pool labeling or its infrastructure template if `worker-d` should be an app node, then recheck coverage.
Change the agent image because image startup controls whether a node counts as eligible.
Add another hostPath volume because each mounted directory increases desired coverage by one.

questions.explanation: The desired count is three because only three nodes match the selector. If `worker-d` should belong to the app pool, its label source is the first repair. DaemonSets do not use a replicas field, and neither image startup nor hostPath count determines node eligibility.

questions.scenario: A new app node has the correct node-pool label, but it also has taint `dedicated=app:NoSchedule`. The log-agent Pod remains Pending, and the DaemonSet template has no tolerations. Which change addresses the scheduling evidence without targeting other node pools?

options.text: Remove the node selector so the agent can run on every node that has spare resources.
Change the taint effect to `PreferNoSchedule` because trusted agents should never declare tolerations.
Add a toleration matching key `dedicated`, value `app`, and effect `NoSchedule` while keeping the app node selector.
Add a replicas field so the scheduler reserves a fourth agent for the tainted node.

questions.explanation: The app selector still chooses the right pool, while a matching toleration lets the trusted agent pass the intentional taint. Removing the selector broadens placement, weakening the taint changes node policy, and DaemonSets do not use replicas to place Pods.

questions.scenario: A review changes the log agent mount from read-only `/var/log/containers` to writable `/` from the host. The agent only needs to read container log files. What is the most accurate review comment?

options.text: Approve it because DaemonSet Pods require writable access to the full node filesystem.
Replace hostPath with a PVC because every node writes its logs into one shared claim by default.
Keep the broad mount but lower the CPU request because resource limits contain filesystem access.
Reject the broader writable mount and keep a focused read-only mount of `/var/log/containers`.

questions.explanation: The article's agent needs only focused read access to the node log directory, so a writable host root mount creates unnecessary risk. DaemonSets do not inherently require full host access, a shared PVC does not expose each node's local logs, and CPU limits do not constrain filesystem permissions.

questions.scenario: A new log-agent image is rolling across four eligible nodes. The DaemonSet shows `DESIRED 4`, `UP-TO-DATE 2`, and `AVAILABLE 3`. One new Pod is crashing, and logs from its node have stopped arriving centrally. Which two actions or settings directly support a controlled response?

options.text: Keep `maxUnavailable: 1` so the rollout limits how many node agents can be absent at once.
Raise `maxUnavailable` to four so every node reaches the crashing image before diagnosis.
Inspect the failed Pod evidence and roll back to the known good revision if telemetry is production-critical.
Delete the DaemonSet selector because unmanaged Pods remain available during rollbacks.

questions.explanation: A one-at-a-time unavailability budget limits coverage loss, and preserving failed-Pod evidence before a prompt rollback supports diagnosis and recovery. Updating every node at once expands the incident, while removing the selector breaks the controller ownership contract.
